titleStmt.title: Eigenvalues of regular symmetric Hall-plates Eigenvalues of regular symmetric Hall-plates

analytic.title: Eigenvalues of regular symmetric Hall-plates Eigenvalues of regular symmetric Hall-plates

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: I discuss uniform, isotropic, plane, singly connected, electrically linear, regular symmetric Hall-plates with an arbitrary number of N peripheral contacts exposed to a uniform perpendicular magnetic field of arbitrary strength. In practice, the regular symmetry is the most common one. If the Hall-plates are mapped conformally to the unit disk, regular symmetry means that all contacts are equally large and all contacts spacings are equally large, yet the contacts spacings may have a different size than the contacts. Such Hall-plates do not change when they are rotated by 360°/N. Their indefinite conductance matrices are circulant matrices, whose complex eigenvalues are computable in closed form. These eigenvalues are used to discuss the Hall-output voltage, the maximum noise-efficiency, and Van-der-Pauw's method for measuring sheet resistances. For practical use, I report simple approximations for Hall-plates with four contacts and 90°symmetry with popular shapes like disks, rectangles, octagons, squares, and Greek crosses with and without rounded corners.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Hall-plates are thin flat pieces of (semi-)conductors with a large mobility of the majority charge carriers. If a magnetic field acts on the carriers the Lorentz force diverts the current streamlines, and this builds up a Hall-electric field E H . The exact solution of the electric field problem in closed analytical form is not trivial, all the more if the Hall-plates have a non-symmetric geometry with extended contacts. Even if the potential in response to a single input current is known everywhere in the Hall-plate, it is still much work to compute the conductance matrix, which relates the voltages at the contacts of the plate to the currents into all contacts. Luckily, all shapes, which are equivalent by conformal transformation, have the same conductance matrix 1 . Therefore, it is sufficient to study simple geometries, like circular disk Hall-plates, because from Riemann's mapping theorem 2 we know that there always exists a conformal transformation that maps the disk to any other singly connected domain. Recently, Homentcovschi and Murray found a general method to compute the conductance matrix of cicular disk Hall-plates without conformal transformation 3 . For singly-connected 2D-domains with N extended peripheral contacts, they defined two matrices, whose entries are numerical integrals that depend on the locations of the vertices of the contacts and on the Hall-angle. One of the matrices needs to be inverted and multiplied by the other one to get the resistance matrix normalized by the sheet resistance. The method is very general, but it does not readily give closed formulae for the resistance matrix of Hall-plates. Yet, for symmetric Hall-plates with N equal contacts the equations can be simplified and one gets closed formulae. This has been done for strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates 4 -they have the highest possible degree of symmetry (see Fig. 1). Instead of computing the resistance matrix directly, it turned out to be simpler to compute the eigenvalues of the indefinite conductance matrix. In this work I apply the same techniques from Ref. 4 to less symmetric Hall-plates -regular symmetric instead of strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates.

This paper starts with the definitions of the indefinite conductance matrix, Ohm's law, and the stream function. The regular symmetry leads to a symmetry in the indefinite conductance matrix-it is a circulant matrix, whose eigenvalues and eigenvectors are computed in closed form in Section III. In Section IV I apply these findings to the theory of Homentcovschi and Murray, to get a closed formula for the complex eigenvalues. Section V discusses some basic properties of these eigenvalues. Section VI shows how to compute the resistance matrix from the eigenvalues. Section VII computes the output voltage of regular symmetric Hall-plates with four contacts. It also gives a very good approximation for the Hallgeometry factor of this most common type of Hall-plate and compares it to formulae given in the literature. Section VIII compares the maximum noise efficiency of regular symmetric versus strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates, and Section IX explains how to generalize van-der-Pauw's method for regular symmetric Hall-plates with four contacts with or without applied magnetic field. The appendix specifies how Hall-plates with popular shapes (Greek crosses, octagons, and rectangles) are equivalent to disk shaped Hall-plates.

div.head: II. DEFINITIONS
div.p: In electrically linear Hall-plates the currents into the terminals are linear combinations of the potentials at the terminals. Suppose that the Hall-plate has N terminals. Let us group all N currents to a vector I and all N voltages to a vector V . Then we can express the linear combination as a matrix multiplication I = i GV , with the indefinite conductance matrix i G. Hereby I use the historic nomenclature in circuit theory 5-8 , where 'indefinite' denotes undefined reference potential (ground node). In a mathematical sense the indefinite conductance matrix is not indefinite, but positive semi-definite. Both indefinite and semi-definite matrices have zero determinants, and therefore i G cannot be inverted. If we ground the ℓ-th terminal, we write I = GV , where we delete the ℓ-th current and voltage in I, V , respectively, and we delete the ℓ-th row and column in i G to get G. The definite conductance matrix of any passive (= dissipative) system is positive definite, its determinant is positive, and an inverse arXiv:2306.14450v1 [cond-mat.mes-hall] 26 Jun 2023 exists: V = RI with the resistance matrix R = G -1 .
In the presence of the Hall-effect Ohm's law is 9
div.formula: E -E H = ρJ with E H = -ρ µ H J × (B z n z ),(1)
div.p: with the electric field, E = -∇φ , being the negative gradient of the electrostatic potential φ , with the current density J , the vector product ×, the unit vector n z orthogonal to the plane Hall-plate, and with the z-component of the magnetic flux density B z . ρ is the homogeneous specific resistivity and µ H is the Hall-mobility. In (1) I use the traditional sign convention for µ H , which means negative Hall-mobility for electrons as majority carriers, and I neglect minority carriers. According to (1) E and J are not colinear -there is the Hall-angle θ H in-between, with tan(θ H ) = µ H B z . I use the following abbreviations,
div.formula: R sheet = ρ t H , R sq = R sheet cos(θ H ) ,(2)
div.p: whereby t H is the thickness of the Hall-plate. For a square plate with two contacts fully covering two opposite edges, the resistance between these contacts is the sheet resistance R sheet at zero magnetic field, and it is the square resistance R sq in the presence of a magnetic field 10 .
In two-dimensional space we can describe the current density J by a stream function ψ via
div.formula: J = -1 ρ ∇ × (ψn z ) = -1 ρ ∂ ψ ∂ y n x + 1 ρ ∂ ψ ∂ x n y ,(3)
div.p: with the nabla operator ∇ and the Cartesian unit vectors n x , n y , n z , the last one being orthogonal to the plane Hallplate. Equation (3) holds only, if current enters and exits the Hall-plate merely through contacts on the single boundary 11,12 . Then the continuity equation ∇ • J = 0 follows immediately from (3) (the dot denotes the scalar product of two vectors). J is orthogonal to the gradient of ψ,

which explains the name 'stream function': it is constant along current streamlines. Along the insulating segments of the boundary there are also specific current streamlines. Therefore, ψ is constant along each insulating segment. Thus, ψ behaves on the insulating boundary segments just like φ behaves on the conducting boundary segments. In the stationary condition Maxwell's second equation is
In the first line of (5) I used (1), in the second line I used n z • ∇ = 0 and ∇ • J = 0, in the third line I used (3), and in the fourth line I used ∇ • n z ψ = 0. Equation (5) says that the
div.formula: I 12 = t H 2 1 J n ds = t H n z • 2 1 (J × t) ds = -t H ρ 2 1 ∂ ψ ∂ x dx + ∂ ψ ∂ y dy = -t H ρ 2 1 dψ = - ψ 2 -ψ 1 R sheet ,(6)
div.p: with
div.formula: J n = J • n = J • (t × n z ) = n z • (J × t).
div.p: The unit vector n is normal to the contour pointing away from the Hall region, and t is the unit vector tangential to the contour pointing from 1 to 2, t = dx n x + dy n y .

div.head: III. SYMMETRY PROPERTIES OF REGULAR SYMMETRIC HALL-PLATES
div.p: Suppose we have a regular symmetric boundary with N contacts of equal size, the spacings between the contacts are all equal, yet they are not necessarily identical to the size of the contacts (see Fig. 1). Suppose we connect an external circuit to this Hall-plate. If we keep the Hall-plate fixed and move the external circuit by one terminal, say clock-wise, then the resulting potentials and currents into the external circuit must remain the same, because the Hall-plate is regular symmetric in shape and the applied magnetic field is parallel to the rotation axis.This means that the entries in the current and voltage vectors move one instance downwards, and the entries in the indefinite conductance matrix move one row down and one column to the right. Both the original and the new i G matrices must be identical. We can repeat this procedure N times. The conclusion is that the elements along diagonals parallel to the main diagonal are identical, which gives the following patterns for i G and G,
div.formula: i G =      A B C D E E A B C D D E A B C C D E A B B C D E A      ⇒ G =    A B C D E A B C D E A B C D E A    . (7
) i G is an N × N matrix, G is an (N -1) × (N -1) matrix.
div.p: We get G from i G by deleting the row and column which corresponds to the grounded contact. G and i G are Toeplitz matrices, which have the property that G ℓ,m = g ℓ-m , which depends only on ℓm. Moreover, i G is a circulant matrix with g ℓ-m = g ℓ-m+N . Third, the sum of entries per row in any indefinite conductance matrix has to vanish, because no currents flow if all contacts are at identical non-zero potential 6-8 . If we map a regular symmetric Hall-plate to the unit disk, its contact C m ranges from azimuthal angles
div.formula: α m = 2π m -1 N to β m = α m + χ π N ,(8)
div.p: for χ ∈ (0, 2) and m = 1, 2, . . . , N. Contacts are small for χ → 0, whereas contact spacings are small for χ → 2. The case χ = 1 gives strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates, whose contacts are equally large as the contacts spacings (see Fig. 1). We can write down the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a real-valued circulant matrix i G, see Ref.
div.formula: 13 i G = Q i ΓQ C with i Γ = diag( i γ 1 , i γ 2 , . . . , i γ N ), (Q) k,ℓ = 1 √ N exp 2πikℓ N ∀k, ℓ ∈ {1, 2, . . . , N}. (9

) Q C is the complex conjugate of Q, and i = √ -1. i Γ is a diagonal matrix, which comprises all eigenvalues i γ ℓ of i G. It holds Q = Q T and QQ C = Q C Q = 1. The eigenvectors of i G are the columns of Q, which are coefficient vectors of the inverse discrete Fourier transform (IDFT). It generally holds i γ N-ℓ = i γ C ℓ ,(10)
div.p: for the eigenvalues of any real circulant matrix, where z C is the complex conjugate of z.

div.head: IV. A CLOSED FORM SOLUTION FOR THE EIGENVALUES OF i G
div.p: We use Ref. 3. Their equation (17) reads in our notation
div.formula: N ∑ m=1 B k,m φ m = N ∑ m=1 -A k,m ψ m cos(θ H ) for k ∈ {1, . . . , N -1}, (11
div.p: whereby φ m is the potential on C m (= the m-th peripheral contact), and ψ m is the stream function on the insulating peripheral segment between C m and C m+1 , with C N = C 0 . In Ref. 3 the entries of the matrices A, B are defined as
div.formula: A k,m = α m+1 β m h(τ) dτ sin((τ -β k )/2) sin((τ -β N )/2) , B k,m = β m α m h(τ) dτ sin((τ -β k )/2) sin((τ -β N )/2) , with h(τ) = N ∏ ℓ=1 sin((τ -β ℓ )/2) sin((τ -α ℓ )/2) (1/2-θ H /π) .(12)
div.p: Note that in (11) the sums extend over N terms, whereas in Ref. 3 they had only N -1 terms. Hence, in contrast to Ref. 3, we do not require φ N = 0 and ψ N = 0. This enables us to work with the indefinite conductance matrix, which is circulant, and therefore it lends for a simpler mathematical treatment than the definite conductance matrix. Therefore, our matrices A, B have N columns and N -1 rows. Equation (11) must hold, if we add an arbitrary constant to all φ k or to all ψ k . Consequently it must hold
div.formula: N ∑ m=1 A k,m = 0 and N ∑ m=1 B k,m = 0 for k ∈ {1, . . . , N -1}. (13
div.p: Multiplying the left equation of ( 13) with ψ N / cos(θ H ) and adding it to (11) gives
div.formula: N ∑ m=1 B k,m φ m = N ∑ m=1 -A k,m ψ m -ψ N cos(θ H ) for k ∈ {1, . . . , N -1}.
div.p: (14) From ( 6) it follows
div.formula: I k = ψ k -ψ k-1 R sheet . (15

div.formula: B(1-1T ) = -R sq AL 1 i G(1-1T ) = -R sq AL 1 (1-1T ) i G,(19)

div.p: Inserting this into (21) gives

div.p: Inserting ( 8) into (12) and using the product formula 14 gives
div.formula: h(τ) = sin χπ 2 cot χπ 2 -cot Nτ 2 (1/2-θ H /π) .(24)
div.p: Back-inserting this into (12) gives after some manipulation
div.formula: A k,m = -2 N (2 -χ) sin χπ 2 × π/2 0 cot χπ 2 -cot χπ 2 + z(2 -χ) (1/2-θ H /π) sin((2-χ)z/N+πm/N) × dz sin((2-χ)z/N+π(m-k)/N) ,(25) and
B k,m = -2 N χ sin χπ 2 × π/2 0 cot χπ 2 -cot (χz) (1/2-θ H /π) sin(χ(2z-π)/(2N)+πm/N) × dz sin(χ(2z-π)/(2N)+π(m-k)/N) .(26)
div.p: Let us insert (25), ( 26) into (23) and use the following identity (see Appendix A)
div.formula: N ∑ k=1 exp (2πikm/N) sin(x/N+πk/N) sin(x/N+π(k-j)/N) = 2N sin (π jm/N) sin (π j/N) (i cot(x)-1) exp im jπ -2x N .(27)
div.p: Finally, this gives the complex eigenvalues i γ m for m ∈ {1, 2, . . . , N} of the indefinite conductance matrix i G of a regular symmetric Hall-plate with N peripheral contacts of size χ as a function of the Hall-angle θ H ,
div.formula: i γ m = -2χi R sq (2 -χ) exp -iπ m N sin π m N × 1 0 1 + i cot π 2 χτ exp iπ χ m N τ cot π 2 χ(1 -τ) -cot π 2 χ (1/2-θ H /π) dτ 1 0 1 -i cot π 2 (2 -χ)τ exp -iπ(2 -χ) m N τ cot π 2 χ -cot π 2 (χ + τ(2 -χ)) (1/2-θ H /π) dτ .(28)

for a = (1 + ε)π/2, b = (1 -ε)y, c = (1 + ε)y. div.p: V. SOME PROPERTIES OF THE EIGENVALUES i γ m A. General Symmetry Properties of i γ m
If we reverse the magnetic field in (32) it holds
div.formula: i γ m (-θ H ) = i γ C m (θ H ) = i γ N-m (θ H ),(33)
div.p: where I used (10) for the rightmost equality. Thus the argument of each eigenvalue is an odd function of the Hall-angle,
div.formula: arg{ i γ m (-θ H )} = -arg{ i γ m (θ H )}.(34)
div.p: Consequently, at zero magnetic field the eigenvalues become real numbers. Moreover, the magnitudes of the eigenvalues are independent of the polarity of the magnetic field
div.formula: i γ m (-θ H ) = i γ m (θ H ) .(35)
div.p: At large positive Hall-angle, θ H → π/2 the integrals in the numerator and in the denominator of (32) grow unboundedly. The h-functions at (-θ H ) are responsible for this. Therefore, we can discard the h-functions at θ H in this asymtotic limit.
Let us first study the two limits
In the second line of (36) the first integral is finite-we can neglect it against the infinite second integral. We use (36) in (32). After a few rearrangements this gives lim
div.formula: θ H →π/2 i γ m = 2 cos(θ H ) R sheet sin π m N exp i π 2 1- 2m N .(37)
div.p: It means that the eigenvalues of the indefinite conductance matrices of regular symmetric Hall-plates at strong magnetic field are proportional to cos(θ H ). They tend to zero, while their arguments are linear functions of m/N, aequidistantly distributed between -90°and 90°. Thereby, the size of the contacts is irrelevant; ε does not show up in (37).
From (32) it also follows
div.formula: i γ m (-ε, -θ H ) i γ m (ε, θ H ) = 2 R sq sin π m N 2 ,(38)
div.p: which is yet another form of the Reverse Magnetic Field on Complementary Device-principle (RMFoCD) 15,16 , relating the eigenvalues of a regular symmetric Hall-plate, i γ m (ε, θ H ), to the eigenvalues of its complementary Hall-plate at reverse magnetic field, i γ m (ε, -θ H ) = i γ m (-ε, -θ H ). A complementary Hall-plate is obtained from an original Hall-plate by swapping all insulating and conducting segments on the boundary. All properties of complementary Hall-plates are denoted by an overbar. In matrix form (38)
div.formula: reads i Γ(-θ H ) i Γ(θ H ) = ∆ R 2 sq ⇒ i Γ(-θ H ) = ∆ R 2 sq i Γ + (θ H ), i Γ = diag( i γ 1 , i γ 2 , . . . , i γ N ), with i γ ℓ (ε) = i γ ℓ (-ε), (∆) k,ℓ = 2 sin πℓ N 2 δ k,ℓ , ∀k, ℓ ∈ {1, 2, . . . , N}.(39)
div.p: ∆ is a diagonal matrix 4 . δ k,ℓ is Kronecker's delta. i Γ is a diagonal matrix. i Γ + is the pseudo-inverse (= Moore-Penrose inverse) of i Γ. In this simple case of a diagonal matrix it means for  0, 0.5, 0.9, 0.99, 0.99999,  10
1.0 (b) For θ H = 3π/8 (strong magnetic field).

FIG. 3: Arguments of the eigenvalues i γ m of regular symmetric Hall-plates versus m/N for various contacts sizes, ε, after (32). that all zeros remain zeros and all non-zeros are replaced by their reciprocals. From (38) and ( 35) we get
div.formula: i γ m i γ m = 2 R sq sin π m N 2 ,(40)
div.p: where we simply wrote i γ m instead of i γ m (ε, θ H ) and i γ m instead of i γ m (ε, θ H ). From (40) it follows
div.formula: i γ m i γ m = R sq i γ m 2 sin π m N 2 = i γ m | i γ m (ε = 0)| 2 .(41)
div.p: From (38) and (34) it follows that the arguments of the original and the complementary Hall-plates are the same,
div.formula: arg i γ m (-ε, θ H ) = arg i γ m (ε, θ H ) .(42)
div.p: Figures 2 and3 show plots of the magnitude and the argument of i γ m versus m/N, for various sizes of contacts, ε, for weak and strong magnetic field. The magnitude becomes smaller with larger Hall-angle, which is plausible, because the conductances get smaller due to the magneto-resistive effect. Moreover, it is also obvious that the magnitudes of the eigenvalues become smaller for smaller contacts, ε < 0, because smaller contacts naturally give smaller conductances, too. Third, the magnitudes tend to zero for m → 0 and m → N, whereas they show a maximum at m = N/2. These properties are reflected in (38). The arguments of the eigenvalues are close to the straight line (1 -2m/N)θ H for very large Hallangles or for almost strictly regular symmetry, |ε| < 0.5. Conversely, at moderate or weak magnetic field, very large or very small contacts pull arg{ i γ m }/θ H towards zero, wherey the sign of ε does not change the arguments of the eigenvalues -small and large contacts with ±ε have the same arg{ i γ m }, see (42).

div.head: B. Eigenvalues i γ m at Zero Magnetic Field
div.p: For even N and θ H = 0 equation (32) gives (see Appendix B)
div.formula: i γ N/2 (θ H = 0) = 2 R sheet * F cos((1-ε)π/4)
div.p: Here, * means a multiplication of two scalars. In (43) we used the incomplete elliptic integral of the first kind, which is available in in Mathematica, F(sin(φ
div.formula: ), k) = EllipticF[φ , k 2 ].
div.p: It is defined by

If N is an integer multiple of three, equation (32) can also be integrated in closed form for m = N/3. However, this gives a long expression for i γ N/3 (θ H = 0), which I do not report here. Later we will get a simpler formula anyhow, see (58).
If N is an integer multiple of four, equation (32) gives (see Appendix B)
wherein K(k) = F(1, k) is the complete elliptic integral of the first kind, also available in Mathematica,
div.formula: K(k) = EllipticK[k 2 ].
div.p: We can get simpler expressions than (43), (45) if we use prior literature on symmetric Hall-plates with three and four contacts, because eigenvalues do not depend on the absolute values of m and N, but only on the ratio m/N, see (32) (this holds with or without applied magnetic field). Therefore, i γ N/2 for even N is identical to i γ 2 for N = 4. Likewise, for N being an integer multiple of four, the eigenvalues i γ N/4 are the same as i γ 1 for N = 4. We use (9) to link the eigenvalues with the conductances for N = 4,
Next, we relate the conductances to the resistances in the equivalent resistor circuit (ERC) according to Ref. 17,

, and A key parameter of Hall-plates with four contacts is the effective number of squares, λ . It is defined as the ratio of the zero magnetic field resistance measured between two nonneighoring contacts over the sheet resistance,
div.formula: C 2 C 1 C 3 C 4 =C 0 R H R H R H r 1,4 =R H r 2,4 =2R D 2R D (a) ERC.
λ = R 2,2 (θ H = 0) R sheet for N = 4.(48)
div.p: A rectangle with end contacts and aspect ratio equal to λ has the same resistance as the four contacts Hall-plate at zero magnetic field. Note that in the ERC of the Hall-plate the resistor r 2,4 = 2R D lies between the contacts C 2 and C 4 , whereas the resistance measured between these contacts is R 2,2 , with R 2,2 ̸ = 2R D . Moreover we define the cross-resistance, R x , measured between two terminals, where the contacts with even numbers are connected to the first terminal and the contacs with odd numbers are connected to the second terminal. At zero magnetic field it holds
div.formula: λ x = R x (θ H = 0) R sheet ,(49)
div.p: with λ x being the number of cross squares. It is straightforward to show how the resistors in the ERC are related to λ , λ x (see also Ref. 18),
div.formula: r 1,4 (θ H = 0) = R H (θ H = 0) = 4R sheet λ x ,and
r 2,4 (θ H = 0) = 2R D (θ H = 0) = 4R sheet λ x λ 4λ x -λ . (50
div.p: From Ref. 18 we also know that λ and λ x are not independent,

div.p: Finally, we express the effective number of squares, λ , by the azimuthal angle, 2θ , subtended by a contact of the Hall-plate when the plate is mapped conformally to the unit disk 20 (see Fig. 4b)

Equations ( 54) and ( 55) are simpler than ( 43) and ( 45). Now we apply the same method to a regular symmetric Hall-plate with three contacts, N = 3. From (9) we get G 1,2 (θ H = 0) = -i γ 1 (θ H = 0)/3. The ERC has only three identical resistors between each pair of contacts. We use Ref. 21, where this resistor is called

div.p: In the limit of vanishing spacings between the contacts, ε → 1, the eigenvalues of ( 54), ( 55) and (58) diverge logarithmically like
Conversely, in the limit of vanishing contacts sizes, ε → -1, these eigenvalues go to zero like 1/K ′ (k → 0) = 1/ ln (4/k).

div.head: C. Eigenvalues for Small Contacts or Contacts Spacings
div.p: Fig. 3 shows that the arguments of the eigenvalues vanish in the limit ε → ±1, except for m/N → 0 or m/N → 1 or θ H → ±π/2, however, they diminish only slowly. Thus, in practical cases |ε| will hardly every be larger than 0.99, and therefore we should not expect to see the arguments vanish in real life.

The strongest deviation of arg{ i γ m }/θ H from the straight line in Fig. 3 occurs in the weak magnetic field limit. There, we may replace z θ H /π → 1 + (θ H /π) ln(z) in (32), analogous to (63) in Ref. 4. This formula is lengthy and seems to give no new insight, therefore I do not report it here.

Regular symmetric Hall-plates with very small contacts spacings, ε → 1, or with very small contacts ε → -1, are linked by (38). Let us study the case of small contacts explicitly-with (38) one can deduce the converse case.

div.head: E. Eigenvalues for Almost Strictly Regular Symmetry
div.p: By almost strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates, we mean regular symmetric Hall-plates with small |ε|, say |ε| < 0.5. Then, according to Fig. 3 the arguments of the eigenvalues are close to a straight line in the plot, which means
div.formula: arg i γ m θ H ≈ 1 -2 m N ,(68)

The largest error occurs near m/N ≈ 0.2 and m/N ≈ 0.8, see Fig. 6. A numerical inspection of the magnitudes of the eigenvalues gives the following approximation (see Figs. 7 and8; see also (41))

div.head: F. Eigenvalues of Regular Symmetric Hall-Plates with a Hole
div.p: Inversion of (9) gives the eigenvalues in terms of the indefinite conductance matrix, see (83). This works also for regular symmetric Hall-plates with holes, because it only relies on the fact that the indefinite conductance matrix is a circulant matrix, regardless if the Hall-plate is a singly-or multiply-connected domain. The conductance matrix of Hallplates with four contacts and a single hole with insulating hole boundary at weak magnetic field is given in Ref. 24. For regular symmetry this gives the eigenvalues
div.formula: i γ 1 = 2 R sheet λ 1+iG (4C1H) H0 tan(θ H ) λ , i γ 2 = 1 λ x R sheet ,(70)
div.p: with λ , λ x as defined in ( 48), (49). Thereby, G (4C1H) H0

is the Hall-geometry factor for regular symmetric Hall-plates with a hole with insulated hole boundary. For singly-connected Hallplates λ x is a function of λ only (see (51)), however, for doubly connected Hall-plates with regular symmetry it becomes a function of λ and of the size of the hole 25 . Then it holds λ /4 < λ x < λ /2.

According to Ref. 24 the complementary Hall-plate has the hole boundary clad with a contact, that is not connected (no current flows in or out of this 'floating' contact, and therefore this contact does not correspond to any row or column in the conductance matrix). For regular symmetry this gives the eigenvalues
div.formula: i γ 1 = 1 R sheet λ +iG (4C1H) H0 tan(θ H ) , i γ 2 = 4λ x R sheet .(71)
div.p: With (C8) and (C9) and with (5.1) from Ref. 24 we can also write
div.formula: i γ 1 = 2 R sheet λ 1+iG (4C1H) H0 tan(θ H ) λ , i γ 2 = 1 λ x R sheet ,(72)
div.p: with G (4C1H) H0
being the Hall-geometry factor of the complementary Hall-plate, whose hole has a floating contact. According to Ref. 24 it holds

Multiplying the eigenvalues of the original and the complementary doubly-connected Hall-plates gives lim
div.formula: θ H →0 i γ m (-θ H ) i γ m (θ H ) = 2 R sheet sin π m N 2 ,(74)
div.p: for m ∈ {1, 2}, N = 4. Thus, at weak magnetic field the eigenvalues of complementary regular symmetric Hall-plates with four contacts are related according to (38), regardless if they have a hole or not.

div.head: VII. REGULAR SYMMETRIC HALL-PLATES WITH FOUR CONTACTS
div.p: The vast majority of modern Hall-plates uses contact commutation schemes 29-32 to cancel out offset errors (= zero-point errors). These schemes rely solely on the RMFR-principle 27,28 , and therefore they work for all electrically linear Hall-plates regardless of their symmetry. However, for the electronic circuits which supply the Hall-plate with power and which amplify its output signal, it is advantageous to use Hall-plates with 90°symmetry (= regular symmetry). Therefore a formula for the output signal of regular symmetric Hall-plates with four contacts is of great practical relevance. The output voltage of an ordinary Hall-plate with four contacts can be expressed as 4 which holds for regular symmetric Hall-plates. In the special case of strictly regular symmetry the prefactor simplifies, 2/(R sq | i γ 1 |) → √ 2. At weak and strong magnetic fields closed formulae for the eigenvalue i γ 1 are given in ( 55), (60), and (37). In Ref. 4 we saw that for strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates arg{ i γ 1 } varies in good approximation quadratically versus the Hall-angle. Now we try the same approxima-tion for regular symmetric Hall-plates.

div.head: VIII. THE MAXIMUM NOISE EFFICIENCY OF REGULAR SYMMETRIC HALL-PLATES
div.p: In Ref. 35 I defined the noise efficiency of Hall-plates by
div.formula: η ′ = SNR |tan(θ H )| 4k b T ∆ f P d . (103
div.p: SNR is the ratio of the output signal over the thermal noise, k b is Boltzmann's constant, T is the absolute temperature, ∆ f is the effective noise bandwidth in which one observes the output signal, and P d is the power dissipated in the Hall-plate.

For optimized patterns of supply voltages or currents and optimized weighting coefficients in the linear combinations of the output signals the maximum noise efficiency at weak magnetic field was found 4
div.formula: η ′ 0max = lim θ H →0 η ′ max = lim θ H →0 tan arg{ i γ 1 } tan(θ H ) = lim θ H →0 arg{ i γ 1 } θ H .(104)
div.p: (104) holds for all Hall-plates, regardless of their symmetry, provided that the eigenvalues are sorted in such a way that i γ 1 has the largest argument. Fig. 12 shows η ′ 0max versus the contacts size of the regular symmetric Hall-plates, ε, for various numbers of contacts. Clearly, the maximum noise efficiency of strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates (in ε = 0) is larger than of regular symmetric Hall-plates with ε ̸ = 0. However, the maximum is flat, and it is all the flatter the more contacts the Hall-plate has. Hence, the noise efficiency of disk-shaped Hall-plates is not much reduced if the size of the contacts deviates slightly from the size of the contacts spacings. The symmetry of the curves for ±ε is a consequence of (42). Note that in the weak magnetic field limit η ′ 0max is the same, regardless if output voltages or currents (or combinations of both) are measured 35 . This is in striking contrast to Refs. 36 and 37, which claim that current mode operation of Hall-plates might have superior noise performance 17,38 .

div.head: IX. VAN DER PAUW'S METHOD FOR REGULAR SYMMETRIC HALL-PLATES WITH FOUR CONTACTS
div.p: Van der Pauw's original method of measuring the sheet resistance R sheet works for plane, uniform, singly connected domains with four peripheral point-sized contacts 39 . His main achievement is to give a single simple equation which relates the sheet resistance to measureable quantities, the so-called trans-resistances. These are voltages between two contacts divided by currents through the other contacts, and they can also be expressed as differences of entries of the resistance matrix,
div.formula: exp π R 3,1 -R 2,1 R sheet + exp π R 3,1 -R 3,2 R sheet = 1.(105)
div.p: Note that for point-sized contacts all entries R ℓ,m of the resistance matrix tend to infinity, yet the trans-resistances R 3,1 -R 2,1 and R 3,1 -R 3,2 remain finite. Van der Pauw's equation ( 105) is nonlinear and needs to be solved numerically for R sheet , whereby R 3,1 -R 2,1 and R 3,1 -R 3,2 are known from two measurements,

The restriction to point-sized contacts brings the big advantages that van der Pauw's equation is independent of the shape of the Hall-plate (as long as it remains singly connected and 13: Circuit for a generalized Van-der-Pauw measurement 18 .
div.formula: I VdP,in V VdP,in V VdP,out C 1 C 2 C 3 C 4 0A × FIG.
div.p: the contacts are at the circumference) and it is also independent of the applied magnetic field (the latter is a consequence of (16c) in Ref. 11). Moreover, the resistances of the leads and of the physical contacts at the interface between the probe tips and the Hall-plate do not cause measurement errorsthis follows from the simple fact that they are in series to the point-sized contacts, which have infinite resistance, anyhow. In practice, the point-sized contacts are also a drawback, because they cause infinite electric field and current density. This leads to velocity saturation, self heating, inhomogeneous temperature, and mechanical stress, which may cause again errors in the measurement of the sheet resistance.

If the contacts are extended, the situation becomes much more complicated, yet one can still generalize van der Pauw's method. The basic idea is to measure the full resistance matrix. If we can express the resistance matrix as a function of the contact geometry, the Hall-angle and the sheet resistance, we can solve this system of equations (at least numerically), provided that this set of nonlinear equations has a unique solution and that there are enough equations.
For regular symmetric geometries with extended contacts at zero Hall angle, θ H = 0, one can use a simple generalized van der Pauw method 18 . Equation (5b) in Ref. 18 becomes
div.formula: L V VdP,in +V VdP,out R sheet I VdP,in 2 = L 2 V VdP,in -V VdP,out R sheet I VdP,in(107)
div.p: whereby the voltages and currents are explained in Fig. 13. Equation ( 107) is a nonlinear implicit equation for R sheet , if we insert numbers for all measured voltages and currents. Let us express the voltages in terms of the resistance matrix R and the input current. This gives
div.formula: V VdP,in +V VdP,out = R 2,2 I VdP,in , V VdP,in -V VdP,out = R 1,1 -R 1,2 + R 1,3 2 I VdP,in ,(108)
div.p: with all resistances evaluated at θ H = 0. Using (99) in (108) and inserting it into (107) gives
div.formula: L 2 R sheet i γ 1 (θ H = 0) 2 = L 4 R sheet i γ 2 (θ H = 0) .(109)
div.p: If we insert ( 43) and ( 45) into (109) it seems astonishing that (109) is valid for all ε ∈ [-1, 1]. Yet, if we insert ( 54) and ( 55) into (109) the identity is obvious in view of the definition of FIG. 14: i γ 2 / i γ 1 and arg i γ 1 for regular symmetric Hall-plates with N = 4 contacts versus contacts size ε and Hall-angle θ H (both functions are independent of R sheet ). The upper row shows 3D-plots, and the lower row shows contour plots. A generalized van-der-Pauw method determines the unique point, where a contour line in the left lower plot crosses a contour line in the right lower plot. Such a crossing point always exists for 1

the modular lambda function L(y) from 51. Equation ( 109) means that at zero magnetic field there is a relation between both eigenvalues and the sheet resistance regardless of the size of the contacts. Now let us turn to the case of regular symmetric Hall-plates with four extended contacts at non-vanishing magnetic field. The electrical properties at the terminals of these plates are defined by three parameters, R sheet , ε, θ H . If we measure the full condutance or resistance matrix, G, R, we can derive all eigenvalues i γ 1 , i γ 2 . Thus, we can say that we 'measure' the eigenvalues. i γ 1 is complex, whereas i γ 2 is real. This gives three equations for the three unknown real numbers. The only question left is, if this set of nonlinear equations has a unique solution, which we show in the sequel by eliminating R sheet from these equations: consider the expressions i γ 2 / i γ 1 and arg i γ 1 . Both are known from the measurements in Fig. 11. They give two equations for two unknowns, ε, θ H -R sheet has dropped out. Fig. 14 shows the 3D-plots and the contour lines of these expressions, from where it is obvious that a unique solution exists for arbitrary values of i γ 2 / i γ 1 and arg i γ 1 . This solution determines ε and θ H , which we insert into (32) to compute i γ 2 R sheet . Finally, comparison with the measured value for i γ 2 gives R sheet . This shows that a generalized vander-Pauw method for regular symmetric Hall-plates can determine the sheet resistance, regardless of the applied magnetic field.
With (38) it follows a symmetry relation of the surface in Fig. 14a,
div.formula: i γ 2 (-ε, θ H ) i γ 1 (-ε, θ H ) i γ 2 (ε, θ H ) i γ 1 (ε, θ H ) = 2. (110
div.p: Thus, for strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates at arbitrary magnetic field it holds
On the other hand, | i γ 2 / i γ 1 | depends stronger on the contacts size ε at zero magnetic field than at non-zero magnetic field. From ( 54) and ( 55) it follows lim ε→-1
div.formula: i γ 2 (θ H = 0) i γ 1 (θ H = 0) = 1, lim ε→1 i γ 2 (θ H = 0) i γ 1 (θ H = 0) = 2.
div.p: We used the asymptotic limit K(x → 1) = ln(4/ √ 1x 2 ) and de l'Hopital's rule. Consequently, the ratio of the magnitudes of both eigenvalues of a four-contacts regular symmetric Hallplate at arbitrary magnetic field is limited
Simultaneously, the argument of i γ 1 is also limited to
div.formula: -θ H 2 ≤ arg{ i γ 1 } ≤ θ H 2 ∀θ H ,(114)
div.p: according to Fig. 14b and (37).

div.head: X. CONCLUSION
div.p: This work discussed plane, uniform, isotropic, singlyconnected Hall-plates with an arbitrary number of N extended contacts and with N-fold symmetry (i.e., transforming the Hall-plate conformally into the unit disk and rotating the disk by integer multiples of 360°/N gives again the same disk).

A special case of great practical relevance is Hall-plates with four contacts and 90°symmetry. The starting point for this paper was a matrix equation from Homentcovschi, Bercia, and Murray 3,40 , which links the potentials to the currents at the contacts of a general uniform singly connected Hall-plate with peripheral contacts. There, I introduced the indefinite conductance matrix, which is a circulant matrix, due to the assumed regular symmetry of the Hall-plates. This admits a closed analytical solution for the complex eigenvalues of the indefinite conductance matrix. The resistance and conductance matrices are sums of weighted eigenvalues. It was shown how the eigenvalues change with contacts size and Hall-angle, and simple approximations were given. The Hall-geometry factor describes how the shape of the Hall-plate affects the magnitude of the output voltage and the nonlinearity of the output voltage versus magnetic field. Exact and approximate formulae for the Hall-geometry factor of plates with four contacts were given and compared to prio art literature. Based on the eigenvalues it was also shown that strictly regular symmetric Hall-plates have better noise efficiency than regular symmetric ones-highest possible degree of symmetry is favorable for low thermal noise. Finally, a generalized van-der-Pauw method for the measurement of the sheet resistance was explained, and it was proven that a unique solution for the sheet resistance exists, regardless of the applied magnetic field. Up to my knowledge this work gives the most comprehensive, simple, and read-to-use body of equations to quantify all electrical parameters of regular symmetric electrically linear Hall-plates at arbitrary magnetic field. This paper is also published in Ref. 56.

div.p: = (-1) jp sin (π jm/N) sin (π j/N) . The main text of this paper discussed the electrical properties (= the eigenvalues) of circular disk-shaped regular symmetric Hall-plates. This appendix shows how to find other wellknown singly connected geometries with peripheral contacts (Greek crosses, octagons, rectangles, squares), which have identical electrical properties (= identical eigenvalues) as the disks. The fundamental idea is, to figure out how large the contacts for these shapes must be, in order to have the same resistance like the disk at zero magnetic field. According to (95) and (97) the weak field magnetic sensitivity depends only on the effective number of squares, λ = R 2,2 (θ H )/R sheet , and all properties at larger magnetic field relate uniquely to the zerofield properties via conformal transformation-consequently, they are also identical to the properties of the disk. Thereby, we can choose λ or λ x , because both are related by (50). In contrast to Versnel 33 I use λ x , because in some cases it leads to simpler conformal mappings: the higher degree of symmetry in the current flowlines allows for splitting up the entire geometry into smaller unit cells with less vertices, and this gives fewer terms in the denominator of the Schwartz-Christoffel transformation. Morever, if two Hall-plate geometries have identical λ x at zero magnetic field, they will also have identical R x /R sheet at any other magnetic field. Hence, we are free to choose the magnetic field (except for θ H = ±π/2 where all finite contact sizes collaps to point contacts and all resistances in the ERC become infinite). Of course, the simplest way is, to use zero magnetic field. Fig. 16 shows how to map the unit cell of the electric potential during the measurement of R x of a disk-shaped Hall-plate from the z-plane to the upper half of the t-plane via the transformation 44

It maps the interior of the circular segment in the z-plane onto the upper half of the t-plane, with z ℓ → t ℓ , (ℓ = 1, . . . , 4). The point z 4 = exp(-iθ ) corresponds to t 4 = -(tan(2θ )) 2 . The bilinear mapping
div.formula: ζ = at + b ct + 1 , with b = 1, a = (tan(θ )) -2 -1 2 , c = (tan(θ )) 2 -1 2 , (C2)

div.p: In the subsequent paragraphs we will map the unit cells of the electric potential during the measurement of R x of other geometries of Hall-plates to the upper half of the ζ -plane, whereby we require the same ζ 2disk as in (C4).

Occasionally, we will make reference to complementary Hall-plates, where contacts and insulating boundary segments are swapped. If a Hall-plate has normalized resistances λ and λ x , the complementary Hall-plate has normalized resistances λ and λ x . The unit cell in Fig. 16b has a normalized resistance 2λ x . If we swap conducting and insulating segments, this unit cell has the normalized resistance 2λ x . If we map the unit cell to the rectangle in the q-plane, it is obvious that the first resistance (2λ x ) is measured between two opposite edges, while the second resistance (2λ x ) is measured between the other two opposite edges. Hence it follows A Greek cross can have end contacts as in Fig. 19 or corner contacts as in Fig. 20a. We call the Hall-plate with end contacts 'original device', and the Hall-plate with corner contacts 'complementary device'. Therefore, the potentials indicated in Fig. 19 occur during the measurement of R x , whereas Fig. 20a shows the potentials at the contacts during a measurement of R x . A quarter of the complementary device in the z-plane is shown in Fig. 20b. It is mapped to the upper half of the ζ ′ -plane in the lower part of Fig. 20b. The resistance between the two contacts in Fig. 20b is also R x . The mapping is
div.formula: 2λ x = 1 2λ x ⇒ λ x = 1 4λ x . (C8
div.p: The shape in the upper part of Fig. 20b is made up of two unit cells. I chose this shape, because its symmetry leads to ζ ′ 1 → ∞, which simplifies the transformation formula in (C10).

div.p: In (C16) a disk with contacts size ε is equivalent to a Greek cross with corner contacts having the filling factor f CC (ε).

The complementary device is a disk with contacts size -ε, which is equivalent to a Greek cross with end contacts having a filling factor 1f CC (ε). Hence, a disk with contacts size ε corresponds to a Greek cross with end contacts with a filling factor f CE (ε) = 1f CC (-ε), which means

For medium-sized contacts we can develop (C16) and (C17) into Taylor series in ε, The relative errors of both approximations in (C18) are less than 1% for -0.6 < ε < 0.7. Fig. 21 shows a plot of (C16), (C17), and (C18). I made a numerical check with finite element simulation by the commercial code from COMSOL Multiphysics. There I used the application mode emdc (= conductive media dc) in two dimensions with Lagrange multipliers (weak constraints: on, constraint type: non-ideal). The Lagrange multipliers are necessary to increase the accuracy of current integration near the singular points at the vertices of the contacts. The mesh had approximately 7 × 10 5 elements per Hall-plate. The sheet resistance was R sheet = 1 Ω, and the conductivity tensor was
div.formula: κ = 1 1 + (tan(θ H )) 2 1 tan(θ H ) -tan(θ H ) 1 .(C19)
div.p: Disk-shaped Hall-plates and Greek crosses with end contacts and with corner contacts were modelled for three cases, case ε θ f CE f CC 1 -1/4 3π/32 0.520614 0.357187 2 0 π/8 0.580980 0.419020 3 5/7 3π/14 0.782991 0.608121 (C20)

A current of 1 A was injected into contact C 2 , while C 0 = C 4 was grounded, and the other contacts C 1 ,C 3 were not connected. The potentials at C 1 ,C 2 ,C 3 were computed for -2 ≤ tan(θ H ) ≤ 2. The potentials of the disks and the Greek crosses differed by less than 500 ppm (see the plots in Figs. 22b and22d and the potentials and the current streamlines in Figs. 22a and22c for cases 1 and 3). The small differences seem to be caused by insufficiently fine meshing, because coarser meshes give larger differences. Nevertheless, this check is a strong indication for the correctness of (C16) and (C17).

div.head: Greek cross with rounded corners
div.p: Fig. 23a shows a Greek cross with rounded corners and end contacts. Compared to the Greek crosses from Appendix C 1 the inner corners are rounded off by quarter circles. The shape is obtained by taking a square with edge length 1 and cutting off quarter disks of radius r Q which are centered at the corners. I call r Q the normalized quarter circle radius, because the edge length is 1. This geometry is one of the best for Hall-plates, because it avoids singularities of the electric field near the corners. Unfortunately, there is no simple conformal transformation of this shape to the upper half of the ζ -plane. Note that the general transformation from these socalled circular arc polygonal domains (CAPDs) or curvilinear polygons is known 47,48 , but it is difficult to find the involved parameters. Alternatively, see Refs. 49 and 50. Yet, for small and moderate r Q one can use the following approximation.

During the measurement of R x a quarter of the Hall-plate is shown in Fig. 23b. It has the resistance 4R x between the contacts on the real and imaginary axes. We map the circumscribing square from the z-plane to the unit disk in the ζ -plane in Fig. 23c via the conformal transformation
This formula is given in Refs. 51 and 52 (it is a simple combination of two mappings, one from the square to the upper half plane, and the second from the disk to the upper half plane).
The constant

This transformation mapps the circle |z| = r Q onto a segment S Q in the ζ -plane, which deviates slightly from a quarter circle (see Fig. 24): the distance from a point on this segment to the origin is smallest, if the point is on the real or imaginary ζaxis, ζ = ζ R , and it increases a bit, when the point moves on the segment S Q towards its center ζ C = |ζ C | exp(iπ/4). Thus, the segment is obtained by streching a quarter circle slightly in the direction ζ = 1 + i. Our approximation replaces the segment S Q by a quarter circle C Q with a radius r ′ , which is in-between these two extremes, ζ R < r ′ < |ζ C |. If we map ζ = r ′ to the z-plane, it will give z = r Q1 > r Q , and if we map ζ = r ′ exp(iπ/4) to the z-plane, it will give z = r Q2 exp(iπ/4) with r Q2 < r Q . We choose r ′ such that the average of r Q1 and r Q2 equals r Q ,
div.formula: r Q ≈ A ′ 2 r ′ 0 dt √ 1+t 4 +exp -iπ 4 r ′ √ i 0 dt √ 1+t 4 . (C23
div.p: For small r ′ we may approximate the integrands by 1t 4 /2, which gives
The final transformation z → log(z) maps the quarter of the annular region in Fig. 23c which has the resistance 4R x = 4λ x R sheet with
div.formula: 4λ x = π/2 | ln(r ′ )| ⇒ r ′ = exp -π 8λ x . (C25)
div.p: Finally, (C25) and (C24) give the cross-resistance R x as a function of the normalized quarter-circle radius r Q

This approximation for r Q is accurate up to 0.4% at r Q = 0.4 (which means λ x = 1.33164 and λ = 3.10440), and even more accurate at smaller r Q (which means smaller λ x and λ ). A Greek cross with rounded corners and end contacts is equivalent to a disk-shaped Hall-plate with contacts size 2θ , if its normalized quarter-circle radius equals

with θ in units of radians in (C28). The cubic fit deviates from (C27) less than ±1% for 11°< 2θ < 76°, and the linear fit has the same accuracy for 12°< 2θ < 67°. The complementary Hall-plate is a Greek-cross with quarter circle contacts (instead of end contacts). If the original disk plate with contacts size 2θ corresponds to a Greek-cross with rounded corners with r Q having end contacts, the complementary disk plate has contacts size 2θ = π/2 -2θ , and it corresponds to the very same Greek-cross shape, i.e. the same r Q , yet with quarter circle contacts instead of end contacts. Hence, for the Greek cross with quarter circle contacts we simply have to replace θ → π/4θ in (C27). It holds

In (C30) we used the overbar to distinguish it from (C27): r Q is the radius of the quarter circle segment in the smooth Greek cross with end contacts, whereas r Q is the radius of the quarter circle segment in the smooth Greek cross with quarter circle contacts. A numerical fit of (C30) gives r Q ≈ 0.07 + 0.61 * θ -0.6 * θ 2 + 0.47 * θ 3 ≈ 0.1 + 0.37 * θ , (C31) They come from the approximation explained in Fig. 24.

with θ in units of radians in (C31). The cubic fit deviates from (C30) less than ±1% for 17°< 2θ < 83°, and the linear fit has the same accuracy for 24°< 2θ < 78°. Fig. 25 shows a plot of (C27), (C28), (C30), and (C31). Equations (C27) and (C30) give the following normalized quarter circle radii for three cases, Numerical checks of these equations, analogous to the ones in the preceding section, are shown in Fig. 26. The mesh had about 1.2 million elements per Hall-plate. The corresponding supply potentials of both Hall-plates in Fig. 26c differ 0.25% at zero magnetic field and 0.11% at tan(θ H ) = 2, and the Halloutput voltages differ 0.42% at tan(θ H ) = 0.05 and 0.22% at tan(θ H ) = 2. The differences in Fig. 26a were smaller: the supply potentials of left and right Hall-plates differ only 143 ppm at zero magnetic field and 72 ppm at tan(θ H ) = 2, and the Hall-output voltages differ 143 ppm at tan(θ H ) = 0.05 and 72 ppm at tan(θ H ) = 2. The reason for the larger discrepancy in the first two Hall-plates is very likely not a numerical inaccuracy of the finite element simulation-instead, it seems to come from the approximations made in (C27) and (C30). Nevertheless, the accuracy is sufficient for practical use, and in view of the approximations, these numerical results corroborate the analytical theory.

div.head: Octagonal Hall-plates
div.p: If a Hall-plate is octagonal, its complementary plate is also octagonal. Let us call the plate in Fig. 27a complementary. The potentials in this figure occur during a measurement of R x . Fig. 27b shows one quarter of the octagonal plate. It has again the resistance R x between its contacts. In Fig. 27c we map it to the upper half of the ζ ′ -plane according to

Numerical checks of (C37), analogous to the ones in the preceding sections, are shown in Fig. 29, where the plots of the potentials versus magnetic field are identical to the ones in the preceding sections.

In practice, ocatgonal Hall-plates are the second best choice (after Greek crosses with rounded corners), because the electric field and the current density have less pronounced singularities at the vertices of the contacts 53 . This leads to smaller electrical non-linearity caused by velocity saturation, and finally, this gives smaller residual offset errors of spinning current schemes.

div.head: Square Hall-plates
div.p: A square Hall-plate can have mid-contacts as in Fig. 30 or corner contacts as in Fig. 31a. We call the Hall-plate with mid-contacts 'original device', and the Hall-plate with corner contacts 'complementary device'. Therefore, the potentials indicated in Fig. 30 occur during the measurement of R x , whereas Fig. 31a shows the potentials at the contacts during a measurement of R x . A quarter of the complementary device in the z-plane is shown in Fig. 31b. It is mapped to the upper half of the ζ ′ -plane in the lower part of Fig. 31b. The resistance between the two contacts in Fig. 31b are also R x . The mapping is given by
The shape in the ζ ′ -plane in Fig. 31b is identical to Fig. 20b. Thus, equations (C11), (C12), and (C13) also hold,
We define the filling factor f SC as the sum of the lengths of all contacts in Fig. 31a over the entire perimeter. Then it holds

This finally gives the filling factor f SC for a square Hall-plate with corner contacts from Fig. 31a with identical properties like a disk-shaped Hall-plate with contacts of size ε from Fig. 16a,

In (C44) a disk with contacts size ε is equivalent to a square with corner contacts having the filling factor f SC (ε). The complementary device is a disk with contacts size -ε, which is equivalent to a square with mid-contacts with the filling factor 1f SC (ε). Hence, a disk with contacts size ε corresponds to a square with mid-contacts of size f SM (ε) = 1f SC (-ε), which gives
For medium-sized contacts we can develop (C44) and (C45) into Taylor series in ε, f SC ≈ 0.684 + 0.36ε -0.08ε 2 + 0.04ε 3 ≈ 0.677 + 0.36ε, f SM ≈ 0.32 + 0.36ε + 0.07ε 2 + 0.035ε 3 ≈ 0.32 + 0.36ε.

For the cubic approximations the relative errors are less than 1% for -0.63 < ε ≤ 1 for f SC and -0.76 < ε < 0.64 for f SM . Fig. 32 shows a plot of (C44), (C45), and (C46). biblStruct.note: 9 There are numerous errors in the literature. The correct formula and its derivation is given in the thesis of Haeusler 22 : Three forces act on moving charges q, the electro-chemical force F e = qE from the battery, the Lorentz force F L = qv D × B, and the brakeforce F b = -|c b |v D . The charge carriers collide with the crystal lattice, therefore F b is anti-parallel to the drift velocity v D , the constant of proportionality is positive, |c b | > 0. For stationary current flow these forces are at equilibrium, F e + F L + F b = 0. Herein, we replace the drift velocity by the current density according to J = qnv D , n being the density of charge carrieres. For negative charge carriers, q < 0, J and v D point in opposite directions. At zero magnetic field this gives the classic Ohm law E = ρJ with ρ = |c b |/q 2 /n > 0. In the presence of a magnetic field the equilibrium of forces gives E + (1/q/n)J × B = ρJ, where the factor 1/q/n is replaced by the Hall-coefficient C H = ρ µ H . Note that q, C H , and µ H are negative for negative charge carriers. The Lorentz force pulls the charge carriers sideways off the current streamlines, until the separation of charges gives a Hall-electric field E H , which counterbalances the Lorentz force, F L + qE H = 0. This means E H = -v D × B = -(1/q/. in German
